name: Servants' Quarters
description: The servants' quarters are a testament to the principle that those who serve empires rarely benefit from them. Narrow bunks line the walls, blankets threadbare and pillows flattened by generations of exhausted heads. A writing desk in the corner holds a dusty diary. Crates are stacked against one wall, and the unmistakable sound of scratching comes from behind them. Something lives in those crates, and it has a lot of legs. Or a lot of friends.

responses.look: A small writing desk with a dusty diary lying open. The diary belongs to the head servant -- the last entry reads: 'Rats ate the flour again. Cook is furious. King dead. Curse spreading. Priorities unclear.' Tucked under the diary is a sheet of official-looking parchment. Blank. But very, very official-looking.
responses.use: You flip through the diary. Most entries concern meal schedules, staff complaints, and an ongoing feud between the laundry department and the kitchen. The final entries grow increasingly panicked as the curse spreads. The blank parchment underneath seems important.
responses.open: You open the diary wider. The spine cracks with the sound of neglected literature. The blank decree underneath shifts into view.
responses.default: You study the desk. It's the only evidence that someone in this castle once cared about record-keeping.
responses.take: The writing desk is built into the wall -- servants' furniture was designed to be functional, not portable. Someone carved their initials in it: 'M.L. was here.' M.L. is probably still here.
responses.talk: You address the writing desk. It does not respond, which puts it on par with most of the castle's administrative staff.
responses.push: You shove the desk. It grinds against the stone floor with a sound that suggests it hasn't moved since the castle was built and resents the implication that it should start now.
responses.pull: You pull the desk toward you. A drawer slides open, revealing a collection of broken quills, dried ink, and a note reading 'STOP STEALING MY QUILLS -- Head Servant.'

responses.look: Wooden crates stacked against the wall, most bearing labels like 'FLOUR,' 'GRAIN,' and 'PROVISIONS.' The scratching from behind them is persistent and organized. Whatever is back there has established a settlement. Opening the wrong crate seems risky.
responses.use: You approach the crates. The scratching intensifies. Something behind the stack is very aware of your presence and not thrilled about it.
responses.push: You push a crate. It's lighter than expected -- whatever was inside has been eaten. The scratching behind it gets louder.
responses.default: You eye the crates warily. They eye you back, metaphorically, through several hundred sets of small, beady eyes.
responses.take: The storage crates are nailed to their shelves, which are nailed to the wall. The servants learned the hard way that loose objects vanish in a castle.
responses.talk: You lean toward the crates and clear your throat. The scratching pauses. Dozens of tiny ears perk up. You have the undivided attention of an entire rodent municipality.
responses.open: The crates are sealed with wax and hammered shut. Someone didn't want casual browsing. A label reads: 'Kitchen surplus -- DO NOT OPEN until feast day.' Feast day was three centuries ago.
responses.pull: You drag a crate away from the wall. The scratching behind it becomes frantic. A pair of beady eyes glare at you from the gap before vanishing into darkness.

responses.look: A row of narrow bunks, each barely wider than a coffin and roughly as comfortable. The blankets are thin, the pillows are flat, and the mattresses have given up any pretense of being soft. Someone has carved 'I QUIT' into the headboard of the nearest bunk, a sentiment apparently shared by the rest of the staff.
responses.take: Too large, too depressing, and too structurally unsound to move. The bunks are staying right where generations of underpaid servants left them.
responses.use: Tempting to nap, but you have a kingdom to save. The mattress seems to understand, sagging in quiet resignation as you move on.
responses.default: You examine the bunk beds. They've hosted the dreams of countless servants, most of whom were probably dreaming about better beds.
responses.talk: You whisper goodnight to the bunks. The silence that answers is the most restful thing these beds have produced in years.
responses.open: The bunks don't open. They're beds, not treasure chests. Though given the state of this castle, the distinction is increasingly academic.
responses.push: You shove a bunk. The frame groans, shifts two inches, and reveals a colony of dust bunnies that have achieved sentience. They scatter.
responses.pull: You pull a bunk away from the wall. Behind it: more wall. The servants didn't even get a secret passage.

responses.look: A small window set in thick stone, its glass grimy enough to turn daylight into a vague suggestion. The view beyond is nothing but a courtyard wall, three feet away. The architect clearly felt servants needed light but not hope.
responses.take: It's set in stone. Literally. The window was here before you and will be here long after you've given up trying to remove it.
responses.use: You peer through the grimy glass -- just a courtyard wall staring back. Inspiring. The window fulfils its minimum obligation to be a window and not a single thing more.
responses.default: You regard the small window. It lets in just enough light to see how depressing the room is, which may not have been the intended design goal.
responses.talk: You ask the window for a better view. It offers the same courtyard wall it's been offering for two hundred years. Consistency is a virtue, apparently.
responses.open: You try to open the window. It's painted shut, rusted shut, and philosophically shut. Three layers of refusal.
responses.push: You push on the glass. It flexes slightly, threatening to crack. The courtyard wall beyond seems to lean in, concerned.
responses.pull: There's no handle, no latch, and no indication this window was ever meant to open. It's less a window and more a transparent wall with delusions of functionality.

responses.look: A sheet of parchment so official-looking it could probably govern a small nation. Which is sort of the point. It bears the watermark of the Royal Paper Mill and has the texture of authority. It's completely blank, which makes it either useless or incredibly dangerous.
responses.use: A blank decree needs a seal to become an official decree. Without one, it's just very fancy scratch paper.
responses.take: The blank decree is pinned to the wall with an ornate brass tack. It's either a deliberate display piece or someone ran out of things to decree and gave up mid-thought.
responses.talk: You address the blank decree. It says nothing, which is both its current state and its primary feature.
responses.open: It's a rolled parchment. You unroll it. Both sides are blank, waiting for someone with the authority -- or audacity -- to write something official on them.
responses.push: You nudge the decree across the desk. Even blank, it moves with more authority than most of the castle's remaining staff.
responses.pull: You slide the decree toward you. The parchment practically hums with bureaucratic potential.

responses.look: A sack of flour that the rats somehow missed. It's buried behind the other crates, sealed tight, and apparently invisible to rodent eyes. A small miracle in a kingdom that could use more of them.
responses.use: The flour is for Martha the Cook. She's been trying to bake with dust and determination for weeks. This sack would make her day.
responses.take: The flour sack has been here so long it's become part of the room's identity. The flour inside has long since turned to chalk. Some things are better left where they petrified.
responses.talk: You address the flour sack. It maintains its composure. Flour has always been the strong, silent type.
responses.open: The sack is sealed tight, and for good reason. Opening it here would be a donation to the rat treasury.
responses.push: You nudge the flour sack. It's heavy and dense -- the good stuff, not the sawdust blend the castle's been surviving on.
responses.pull: You tug the sack toward you. It's wedged behind the crates, and the rats' scratching intensifies at the disturbance.

actions.text: You slide the blank decree from under the diary. The parchment practically hums with potential. A blank official document in a kingdom without a king -- this is either a bureaucratic emergency or an opportunity for creative governance. Your mother definitely would not approve.

You set the rat trap behind the crates. The scratching stops. A moment of silence. Then: SNAP. A chorus of panicked squeaking erupts as the rat population realizes their reign has ended. Within minutes, the survivors have fled through tiny holes in the wall, leaving behind a scene of crumbs, droppings, and one unprotected sack of flour. The rat hierarchy has been toppled. Democracy reigns among the remaining vermin.

You set the rat trap near the crates. SNAP. The rats scatter in a furry, squeaking exodus. The flour sack behind the crates is now accessible, and the servants' quarters smell marginally less of rodent. Progress.

With the rats gone, you grab the flour sack. It's heavy, dry, and the most valuable thing in this room -- which says more about the room than the flour. Martha in the kitchen will be ecstatic. Or at least marginally less murderous.

You reach toward the flour sack. The scratching behind the crates reaches a fever pitch. Dozens of tiny eyes gleam in the shadows. The rats have established a perimeter around the flour supply and they are not accepting visitors. You'd need to deal with the rat problem first.

rat-swarm.title: Death by Vermin
rat-swarm.narratorText: You open a crate. The scratching stops. A moment of terrible silence. Then they pour out -- a torrent of rats, organized, numerous, and deeply territorial. They've been running this storage room for weeks, and they do not recognize your authority. The swarm engulfs you with the efficiency of a well-managed operation. The rats have a hierarchy, a delegation system, and apparently a policy regarding intruders. You have been outvoted by approximately four hundred to one.

dynamicDescriptions.rats_caught: The servants' quarters are quieter now, the rat problem resolved with extreme prejudice. Crates sit open and rat-free, the flour sack accessible behind them. The remaining rodent population has relocated to parts of the castle that are Someone Else's Problem.

tiers: Those crates are making an awful lot of noise. Something is living behind them, and it's not paying rent. If only there were a device designed specifically for this problem.
A rat trap would clear out the rodents infesting these crates. The castle garden's gardener shed has one -- rusty but functional. Rats respond to mechanical persuasion.
Get the rat trap from the gardener's shed in the castle garden, then use it on the storage crates here. The rats will scatter, giving you access to what's behind them.
There's something useful hidden behind those crates, but the current tenants are rather protective of it. Deal with the pest problem first.
The flour sack is behind the storage crates, but the rats are guarding it. Once you've dealt with the rat problem, you'll be able to reach it. Martha in the kitchen would love some flour.
After using the rat trap on the crates to clear the rats, take the flour sack. Bring it to Martha the Cook in the royal kitchen -- she'll reward you with a skeleton key.
